title: Backbone Families at a Glance
concept: Backbone families represent different design tradeoffs: some are simple or historical, some favor depth and trainability, some emphasize feature reuse, and others are built for mobile efficiency.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what does the backbone of a vision model mainly do?

options.text: Extract useful visual features that later task-specific layers can use
Store labels in alphabetical order only

exercises.explain: Right — now the question becomes: which backbone family should you pick?

cards.title: A toolbox, not one magic hammer
cards.body: When someone says “use a ResNet backbone” or “try MobileNet,” they are pointing to a family of models with a recognizable design style. These families exist because computer vision has many different needs: teaching, benchmarking, fast deployment, edge devices, and stronger feature reuse.

cards.title: A backbone family is a tradeoff family
cards.body: A backbone family is not just a list of names. It is a design philosophy with a typical tradeoff point. LeNet and AlexNet matter historically. VGG is simple and uniform but heavy. ResNet makes deep models easier to train. DenseNet pushes feature reuse. MobileNet is mobile-first. EfficientNet aims for balanced scaling.

cards.title: Different vehicles for different roads
cards.body: A bicycle, pickup truck, and city bus all move people, but they fit different roads and jobs. Backbone families are like that. They all extract visual features, but they do it with different priorities: simplicity, depth, reuse, or efficiency.

cards.title: What a family name hints at
cards.body: If a teammate says “use a ResNet backbone,” that already hints at skip connections and strong general-purpose features. “Use MobileNet” hints at a lighter model designed for mobile hardware. “Use EfficientNet” hints at an efficiency-minded design with more balanced scaling. The family name gives a first guess about cost and behavior.

cards.title: Newer does not mean automatically better for every job
cards.body: There is no universal ranking that says one family wins forever. A VGG-style backbone might be easy to understand in a lesson. A ResNet might be a solid default. A MobileNet may be smarter for a phone app. The right family depends on what you need, not just on which paper came later.

cards.title: A quick map of common families
cards.body: These families occupy different places in the tradeoff landscape.

left.text:
LeNet, AlexNet: historical stepping stones
VGG: simple but heavy
ResNet, DenseNet: stronger deep feature extractors

right.text:
MobileNet: mobile-first design
EfficientNet: balanced scaling
Built to care more about compute budget

exercises.prompt: Which family is most directly associated with skip connections that made very deep CNNs easier to train?
exercises.explain: Correct — ResNet is the classic skip-connection backbone family.

exercises.prompt: True or false: backbone families mainly represent different design tradeoffs rather than one permanent best ranking.
exercises.explain: True — the family name tells you what tradeoff style you are likely getting.

exercises.sentence: A backbone family is a design ___ with recurring tradeoffs, not just a random list of models.
exercises.explain: Exactly — the family groups models by their design instinct.

exercises.prompt: Which quick summary sounds most correct?

options.text: VGG is known for simple repeated blocks, ResNet for skip connections, and MobileNet for mobile efficiency
All backbone families are basically identical except for their logos
EfficientNet matters only because it changed image file formats

exercises.explain: Yes — that summary captures three families by their real tradeoff themes.

exercises.prompt: You need a first backbone candidate for a battery-powered phone app, and you want something explicitly designed to respect tighter compute budgets. Which family is the best first place to look?

options.text: MobileNet or EfficientNet-style families, because they were designed with efficiency in mind
VGG only, because heavier uniform stacks are always the best for phones
LeNet only, because historical models always dominate modern deployments
Any family at random, because hardware constraints never affect backbone choice

exercises.explain: Exactly. Families sit at different tradeoff points, and efficiency-minded families are usually better starting points for tight hardware. Next: how do you choose between accuracy and speed?
